html_content:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Revenue scaling from ₹373 Cr to ₹5,010 Cr drove fixed-cost absorption and transformed gross margins from -5.4% to 13.2%, but the reverse is now true: with FY25 revenue declining 9.9% and Futurefactory under-utilised at 1M capacity, fixed costs are crushing profitability.

Operating Leverage Gains

---

The Trend

| Metric | FY22 | FY23 | Q1 FY24 |
|--------|------|------|---------|
| Revenue (₹ Cr) | 373 | 2,631 | 1,243 |
| Employee Cost / Revenue | 61.9% | 15.3% | 7.4% |
| Ad Spend / Revenue | 10.8% | — | 1.0% |
| R&D Spend / Revenue | 38.5% | 18.3% | 7.3% |
| Asset Turnover | 0.34 | — | 2.68 (ann.) |
| Adj. Gross Margin | -5.4% | 7.6% | 13.2% |

---

What's Driving It

1. Fixed Cost Absorption

As revenue scaled from ₹373 Cr → ₹5,010 Cr, fixed costs (factory, corporate, R&D infrastructure) were spread across more units.

2. Gen 2 Platform Efficiency

68.85% component commonality reduces:
  • Manufacturing complexity
  • Inventory requirements
  • Supplier management overhead
  • Per-unit R&D amortization

3. In-House Manufacturing

  • Battery packs, motors, frames made in-house
  • Eliminates supplier margins on key components
  • Started motor + frame manufacturing Aug 2023

4. D2C Efficiency

  • Brought experience centres in-house (Jul 2023)
  • Eliminated ₹700 Cr+ annual fee to Ola Fleet Technologies
  • Digital-first model reduces per-sale distribution cost

---

The Problem

Operating leverage works in both directions. With FY25 revenue declining 9.9%, fixed costs become a burden again:


  • Factory under-utilization (1M capacity, lower volumes)

  • R&D spend must continue regardless of sales

  • Experience centre costs are largely fixed

---

Data Sources

DATA: Source: QUARTERLY_FINANCIALS_EXT.csv
EBITDA margins swung from -22.1% (Q4 FY25 trough) to +4.5% (Q4 FY26) as OpEx was slashed 42% (₹840 Cr → ₹484 Cr) — the breakeven dropped from ~40K to ~15K units/month.
DATA: Source: PRODUCT_MIX_QUARTERLY.csv
Gross margin more than doubled from 18% (Q1 FY25) to 36% (Q4 FY26) even as blended ASP fell 33% — Gen 2 platform (68.85% commonality) + Gigafactory cells drove the improvement.
DATA: Source: E2W_MONTHLY_REGISTRATIONS.csv
Monthly registrations collapsed from 53,320 (Mar 24) to 7,567 (Nov 25) — the 1M-unit factory capacity was sized for a demand level that disappeared after the service crisis.
DATA: Source: GIGAFACTORY_MILESTONES.csv
Gigafactory at 6 GWh with only ~16% utilisation (39K units/quarter consumes ~0.5 GWh). Each kWh carries disproportionate depreciation (₹566 Cr/yr, Mar 25 P&L) and interest (₹366 Cr/yr).
DATA: Source: PROFIT_AND_LOSS.csv
Ola's selling and admin expenses ballooned from Rs 58 Cr (FY21) to Rs 1,598 Cr (FY25) — a 27.5x increase in 4 years — while sales grew from Rs 0.86 Cr to Rs 4,514 Cr. The SG&A-to-sales ratio deteriorated from 67:1 (FY21) to 35% of revenue (FY25). This cost structure was built for the 50%+ market share era and became unsustainable when share collapsed.
Causal chain: High-growth spending→fixed cost build-up→volume collapse→SG&A unsustainable→restructuring required→breakeven lowered→extreme cost cuts implemented
Cells: Row 2 Sales: Mar-21=0.86, Mar-25=4514; Row 7 Selling and admin: Mar-21=58.06, Mar-25=1598
DATA: Source: PROFIT_AND_LOSS.csv
Ola's employee cost grew from Rs 197 Cr (FY21) to Rs 465 Cr (FY25) — only 136% growth, far slower than revenue growth (5,248x), demonstrating operating leverage in fixed costs. As % of sales, it dropped from 22,935% to 10.3%. Despite 56% revenue decline from peak, employee base was not significantly reduced, suggesting retention of Gigafactory and R&D talent.
Causal chain: Fixed employee base was leveraged across growing sales, dramatically reducing employee cost as % of revenue; strategic retention of Gigafactory talent even during crisis
Cells: Row 6 Employee Cost: Mar-21=197.25, Mar-23=429.72, Mar-24=450.6, Mar-25=465
DATA: Source: Ola_after_ipo.txt
OpEx was slashed from Rs 840 Cr (Q4 FY25 peak) to Rs 484 Cr (Q3 FY26) — a 42% reduction in two quarters. Achieved by firing thousands of sales staff, closing underperforming experience centers, and cutting marketing spend drastically. This is what drove the EBITDA breakeven threshold from 40K to 15K units/month.
Causal chain: The drastic OpEx reduction was a survival move: revenue had collapsed and costs had to follow; this drove the EBITDA breakeven threshold from 40K to 15K units/month
Cells: Section 3: Cost cutting details
DATA: Source: QUARTERLY_FINANCIALS_EXT.csv
Mgmt Primary Focus shifted dramatically across 8 quarters: Q1 FY25 'Scale and Market Dominance' → Q2 'IPO Roadshow' → Q3 'Defending Price Wars' → Q4 'Crisis Management & Hyperservice' → Q1 FY26 'Cost Cutting & OpEx Reduction' → Q3 FY26 'Structural Profitability & LFP Pivot' → Q4 FY26 'B2B Battery Supply & Margin Defense'. A complete strategy reset from aggressive growth to survival.
Causal chain: Management focus went from aggressive growth to survival to restructuring to profitability to B2B diversification in just 8 quarters — a complete strategy reset
Cells: Row 2-9: Col G (Mgmt_Primary_Focus)
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/Ola_after_ipo.txt
OpEx was slashed from Rs 840 Cr (Q4 FY25 peak) to Rs 484 Cr (Q3 FY26) — a 42% reduction in two quarters. Achieved by firing thousands of sales staff, closing underperforming experience centers, and drastically cutting marketing spend. This is what drove the EBITDA breakeven threshold from 40K to 15K units/month.
Context: Section 3: Financial Restructuring — The paradox explained
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/Ola_after_ipo.txt
The drastic OpEx reduction was a survival move: revenue had collapsed and costs had to follow. The new EBITDA breakeven at 15,000 units/month (down from ~40,000+ previously) means Ola is now EBITDA-positive at just 39% of its peak quarterly volume (39K units in Q4 FY26 vs 108K peak).
Context: Section 3: Financial Restructuring and Section 5: Strategic Implications
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP employee benefits expense: Rs 197.25 Cr (FY21), Rs 282.48 Cr (FY22), Rs 426.73 Cr (FY23), Rs 94.39 Cr (Q1 FY24). As a % of total income: 185.94% (FY21), 61.91% (FY22), 15.33% (FY23), 7.38% (Q1 FY24) -- demonstrating significant operating leverage as revenue scaled.
Context: Page 327, Summary Results of Operations
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP employee cost composition (Q1 FY24): Salaries, wages and bonus Rs 59.91 Cr (Rs 599.12M), equity-settled share-based payments Rs 23.11 Cr (Rs 231.06M), staff welfare expenses Rs 5.95 Cr (Rs 59.52M). FY23 share-based payments: Rs 110.05 Cr (Rs 1,100.52M), up from Rs 19.54 Cr (Rs 195.37M) in FY22.
Context: Pages 328, 330
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP R&D spend as % of total income decreased from 38.54% (FY22) to 18.25% (FY23) to 7.32% (Q1 FY24), demonstrating economies of scale as revenue grew faster than R&D investment.
Context: Page 320
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP advertising, marketing and sales promotion expenses as % of total income decreased from 10.83% (FY22) to 1.03% (Q1 FY24), reflecting disciplined and targeted approach to marketing spend.
Context: Page 319
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP asset turnover ratio (revenue from operations divided by total fixed and intangible assets) improved from 0.34 in FY22 to 2.68 annualized in Q1 FY24, reflecting capital productivity improvement.
Context: Page 320-321
DATA: User: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP selling and distribution expenses (fee paid to Ola Fleet Technologies for experience centre operations): Rs 35.31 Cr (FY22), Rs 72.26 Cr (FY23), Rs 34.55 Cr (Q1 FY24). Fee eliminated from Jul 1, 2023 when Ola assumed direct control of centre operations.
Context: Page 316
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP total other expenses: Rs 87.65 Cr (FY21), Rs 410.43 Cr (FY22), Rs 886.24 Cr (FY23), Rs 256.84 Cr (Q1 FY24). Includes selling & distribution, warranty, research cost, technology cost, legal & professional fees, and advertising.
Context: Page 327
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/financial_mda.txt
DRHP employee strength: 3,733 on-roll employees and 2,844 off-roll employees as of October 31, 2023.
Context: Page 315

Connections

Causes: Strategy/Backward Integration, Strategy/D2C Omnichannel

Effects: Strategy/Product_Mix_Cannibalization

Amplifiers: Technology/Gen 2 Platform, Technology/Gigafactory_Updates

Linked Nodes: Technology/R&D Investment, Strategy/EV Hub Tamil Nadu
